scenarios.label: Public timeline
scenarios.rdbSummary: A chronological list of public rows is exactly what an RDB does well: one index on created_at, a simple visibility predicate, and no joins.
scenarios.graphSummary: The graph materializes `IN_PUBLIC_FEED` edges from every public Post to a dedicated Feed vertex at seed time. Gleaph's labeled-edge insertion order becomes the chronological key, so a single fixed-label expansion with `LIMIT` returns newest-first without an explicit `ORDER BY`. The value is not speed; it is that the same vertex model will later power relationship-aware feeds without a new schema.
scenarios.preparedQuery: MATCH (feed:Feed {demo_id: 16})<-[:IN_PUBLIC_FEED]-(p:Post)<-[:POSTED]-(author:User) RETURN p.demo_id AS post_id, author.name AS author_name, p.body AS body, p.created_at AS created_at ORDER BY p.order_index DESC LIMIT 20

scenarios.label: Alice home feed
scenarios.rdbSummary: An RDB needs a follows join table and a two-hop join (follower → followee → posts). The query is still SQL-shaped, but the relationship is now a traversal, not a foreign-key lookup.
scenarios.graphSummary: Gleaph materializes `IN_HOME_FEED` edges from every public Post to each follower User at seed time, so Alice's feed is a single bounded expansion from her user vertex. The feed is the natural result of the relationship shape, not an application-side assembly, and the default descending labeled-edge scan returns newest posts first with no runtime sort key.
scenarios.preparedQuery: MATCH (u:User {demo_id: 1})<-[:IN_HOME_FEED]-(p:Post)<-[:POSTED]-(author:User) WHERE p.is_public = TRUE RETURN p.demo_id AS post_id, author.name AS author_name, p.body AS body, p.created_at AS created_at ORDER BY p.order_index DESC LIMIT 20

scenarios.label: Topic path
scenarios.rdbSummary: Proving why a post was recommended requires re-running and documenting the joins: follow, post, topic. The answer is scattered across tables.
scenarios.graphSummary: Gleaph returns the same result plus the edge identities that caused it: alice-follows-bob, bob-posted-1, post-bob-1-topic-graph. The path is part of the query result.
scenarios.preparedQuery: MATCH (p:Post)-[has_topic:HAS_TOPIC]->(t:Topic) WHERE t.demo_id = 7 MATCH (u:User)-[follows:FOLLOWS]->(author:User)-[posted:POSTED]->(p) WHERE u.demo_id = 1 RETURN p.demo_id AS post_id, author.name AS author_name, follows.demo_edge_id AS follows_edge_id, posted.demo_edge_id AS posted_edge_id, t.demo_id AS topic_id, has_topic.demo_edge_id AS topic_edge_id, p.body AS body, p.created_at AS created_at ORDER BY p.order_index DESC

scenarios.label: Semantic discovery
scenarios.rdbSummary: Pure vector search has no join table: it scores every public Post against a fixed query vector and returns the nearest neighbors by L2-squared distance.
scenarios.graphSummary: Gleaph stores canonical Post embeddings on the Graph shard and routes vector SEARCH through the derived index. `post-dave-1` is deliberately the nearest public Post even though Alice does not follow Dave.
scenarios.preparedQuery: MATCH (p:Post)<-[:POSTED]-(author:User) WHERE p.is_public = TRUE SEARCH p IN (VECTOR INDEX post_vec FOR $query LIMIT 10) DISTANCE AS distance RETURN p.demo_id AS post_id, author.name AS author_name, p.body AS body, distance ORDER BY distance ASC

scenarios.label: Alice semantic feed
scenarios.rdbSummary: Combining vector similarity with a relational filter requires joining the vector result set back to followee authorship, usually in application code.
scenarios.graphSummary: Gleaph applies the same vector SEARCH inside the graph pattern `Alice → FOLLOWS → author → POSTED → Post`. `post-dave-1` is excluded despite being nearer because Dave is not a followee; only Bob and Carol's posts are returned, in semantic order.
scenarios.preparedQuery: MATCH (u:User)-[:FOLLOWS]->(author:User)-[:POSTED]->(p:Post) WHERE u.demo_id = 1 AND p.is_public = TRUE SEARCH p IN (VECTOR INDEX post_vec FOR $query LIMIT 10) DISTANCE AS distance RETURN p.demo_id AS post_id, author.name AS author_name, p.body AS body, distance ORDER BY distance ASC
